community-event-registration-cleaner.label: Final Capstone: Community Event Registration Cleaner
community-event-registration-cleaner.summary: A community center receives messy registration exports. Build a modular cleaner that preserves valid registrations, summarizes seats by event, and writes dependable output files.

sketch0.title: A new domain, the same engineering habits

cp_4.title: Move cleaning into a helper module

try-community-event-registration-cleaner.title: Clean one registration preview
try-community-event-registration-cleaner.prompt: Read the first data row from `data/registration.csv`. Split the four fields, title-case the name, convert seats to an integer, lowercase the email, and print `Ava Smith reserved 2 seat(s) for Python Night <ava@example.com>`.
try-community-event-registration-cleaner.hint: Skip the header before splitting the first data row.

help.concept: A small preview verifies the input shape before the larger capstone adds error handling and modules.
help.hint_1: Clean each field after splitting the row.
help.hint_2: Use the values from the file instead of hard-coding the final line.

try-community-event-registration-cleaner.starterCode:
with open("data/registration.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    row = next(file).strip()

# Split the row, clean name/email, convert seats, and print the required line.

main_py.content:
with open("data/registration.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    row = next(file).strip()

name, event, seats, email = row.split(",")
print(f"{name.strip().title()} reserved {int(seats)} seat(s) for {event.strip()} <{email.strip().lower()}>")

cp-2-clean-one-registration.title: Clean one registration row
cp-2-clean-one-registration.prompt: Continue the community-event registration cleaner. In step 2 of 6, keep the file-preview context, then add `clean_row(line)`. Return a dictionary with title-cased `name`, stripped `event`, integer `seats`, and lowercase `email`. Read the first data row and print the cleaned dictionary.
cp-2-clean-one-registration.hint: Make one row dependable before processing the complete file.

help.concept: A single-row helper creates one tested contract for every later row.
help.hint_1: Split into four names before cleaning each field.
help.hint_2: Run the complete supplied dataset and compare every output line with the expected result.

cp-4-move-cleaning-to-module.title: Move cleaning into a helper module

cp-6-write-final-reports.title: Write the final registration reports
cp-6-write-final-reports.prompt: Continue the community-event registration cleaner. In step 6 of 6, create `tools/reports.py`. Write cleaned records to `output/clean_registrations.csv`, write the seat and event summary to `output/summary.txt`, call both helpers from `main.py`, and print the final deterministic summary.
cp-6-write-final-reports.hint: The final step should leave both output files and a concise console handoff.

help.concept: A finished data tool produces durable artifacts and reports enough information to verify the run.
help.hint_1: Create output/ through pathlib before opening the report paths.
help.hint_2: Run the complete supplied dataset and compare every output line with the expected result.

cp-6-write-final-reports.starterCode:
from tools.cleaning import clean_row
from tools.reports import write_clean_registrations, write_summary
from tools.summary import event_counts, total_seats

records = []
with open("data/registrations.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    for line in file:
        try:
            records.append(clean_row(line))
        except ValueError:
            continue

total = total_seats(records)
counts = event_counts(records)
write_clean_registrations(records)
write_summary(total, counts)

print(f"Cleaned {len(records)} registrations")
print(f"Total seats: {total}")
for event, count in sorted(counts.items()):
    print(f"{event}: {count}")

from tools.cleaning import clean_row
from tools.reports import write_clean_registrations, write_summary
from tools.summary import event_counts, total_seats

records = []
with open("data/registrations.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    for line in file:
        try:
            records.append(clean_row(line))
        except ValueError:
            continue

total = total_seats(records)
counts = event_counts(records)
write_clean_registrations(records)
write_summary(total, counts)

print(f"Cleaned {len(records)} registrations")

for event, count in sorted(counts.items()):

tools_reports_py.content:
from pathlib import Path


def write_clean_registrations(records, path="output/clean_registrations.csv"):
    output_path = Path(path)
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with output_path.open("w") as file:
        file.write("name,event,seats,email\n")
        for record in records:
            file.write(f"{record['name']},{record['event']},{record['seats']},{record['email']}\n")


def write_summary(total, counts, path="output/summary.txt"):
    output_path = Path(path)
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with output_path.open("w") as file:
        file.write(f"Total seats: {total}\n")
        for event, count in sorted(counts.items()):
            file.write(f"{event}: {count}\n")

cp-6-write-final-reports.solutionCode:
from tools.cleaning import clean_row
from tools.reports import write_clean_registrations, write_summary
from tools.summary import event_counts, total_seats

records = []
with open("data/registrations.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    for line in file:
        try:
            records.append(clean_row(line))
        except ValueError:
            continue

total = total_seats(records)
counts = event_counts(records)
write_clean_registrations(records)
write_summary(total, counts)

print(f"Cleaned {len(records)} registrations")
print(f"Total seats: {total}")
for event, count in sorted(counts.items()):
    print(f"{event}: {count}")

try_community_event_registration_cleaner.title: Try it yourself
try_community_event_registration_cleaner.prompt: Read the first data row from `data/registration.csv`. Split the four fields, title-case the name, convert seats to an integer, lowercase the email, and print `Ava Smith reserved 2 seat(s) for Python Night <ava@example.com>`.

sketch-1.title: A new domain, the same engineering habits
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
The module project cleaned student scores. The capstone deliberately changes the domain and the record shape: a community center needs attendee names, event names, seat counts, and email addresses cleaned from a registration export.

You will inspect the file, make one row dependable, skip invalid seat counts, move cleaning and summaries into helper modules, and write two final artifacts. The workflow reuses the course skills without copying the earlier school-record project.
